intro:
Corinth (meaning: which is satisfied; ornament; beauty) was one of the most important cities of the ancient Mediterranean world, situated on the narrow isthmus connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese. Its strategic position gave it command over two major harbors — Cenchreae on the east (Aegean) and Lechaion on the west (Adriatic) — making it a center of trade connecting east and west. The Romans destroyed Corinth in 146 BC and refounded it as a Roman colony in 44 BC, after which it rapidly became the capital of the Roman province of Achaia and one of the empire's most cosmopolitan cities.

Paul spent eighteen months in Corinth on his second missionary journey (Acts 18:11), founding the church there and writing two of his major letters to its community. The city's religious life was notably diverse, with temples to Apollo, Aphrodite, and numerous other deities, and its population mixed Greek, Roman, Jewish, and Eastern elements. The moral challenges Paul addresses in his Corinthian letters — divisions, lawsuits, sexual immorality, disputes over spiritual gifts — reflect the complex social environment of a prosperous, multicultural port city. Corinth remained a significant Christian center throughout the patristic era.
